title: Chapter 1
html:
Foreword

When William Congreve’s The Way of the World appeared in 1700, it did so at the moment when the spirit of the Restoration, with all its libertine vivacity and sparkling wit, had reached its last shimmering afterglow before yielding to the sober, moralizing currents of the eighteenth century. To speak of this comedy is to speak of the twilight of an age, one in which language itself became the supreme instrument of human commerce, not merely in business or law but in the theater of erotic and intellectual life. The Restoration comedy, already chastened by shifting tastes and mounting criticism, reached with Congreve a refinement so absolute that it could hardly be surpassed, and so, by the law of aesthetic finality, it became in itself an epitaph for the genre. That the play was not immediately adored by its first audience has the piquancy of irony, for in hindsight it is the crowning jewel of its tradition, a work in which the polished cruelty of wit becomes indistinguishable from the condition of being human.

Congreve’s genius resided in his capacity to transmute the cynical hedonism of his predecessors into a grammar of desire that was at once lyrical and surgical. If Wycherley, in The Country Wife, embodied the audacity of eros in its most unrepentant form, Congreve in The Way of the World distilled that boldness into a structure of verbal dueling, where the true conquests are made not in the bedroom but in the syllables that precede it. The Restoration stage had always been a theater of masks, of mannered seductions and concealed betrayals, yet Congreve reconfigured this masquerade into a meditation on language as the very arena in which power is enacted and undone. His lovers do not merely woo; they spar, they test, they refine one another into sharper and more perilous forms of selfhood.

In its historical moment, the play stands at the intersection of the fading libertine ethos and the rise of bourgeois morality. The Glorious Revolution had stabilized the monarchy, but also signaled the triumph of a more measured civic order. The stage, always a mirror of its society, found itself under scrutiny from moral reformers who found Restoration wit too lubricious, too destabilizing. Congreve, aware of this climate, offered not a capitulation but a transformation. He clothed libertinism in elegance so crystalline that the objection to it must be made in the name of Puritanical severity, and in doing so he safeguarded the comedy of manners by elevating it to art. His play, rejected in part by its own contemporaries, now reads as the inevitable flowering of the Restoration’s aesthetic: not the exuberant shock of Wycherley, not the cheerful amorality of Etherege, but a perfection that exhausts its own tradition.

It is necessary to dwell on the play’s style, for Congreve’s sentences are themselves the substance of his drama. One could say, with only slight exaggeration, that in Congreve the plot is little more than scaffolding for dialogue, a series of excuses to orchestrate the clash and fusion of verbal intelligence. The Restoration stage lived by wit, but Congreve’s wit is of a particular severity, a wit conscious of itself as the essence of human interaction. Each exchange is poised on the edge of cruelty, yet it is cruelty sublimated into form, into cadence, into the rhythm of repartee. The comedy is not in jokes but in the recognition that language itself is both weapon and shield, the sole means by which individuals assert and defend their being in a world that is itself a stage.

What, then, is Congreve’s contribution to the Western canon? He is the poet of manners as metaphysics. In The Way of the World, social convention is not a backdrop but the very stuff of ontology. Just as Dante’s Florence, with its mercantile intrigues, provided the soil for his cosmic allegory, so Congreve’s London salons and drawing rooms become the cosmos within which human souls reveal themselves. The intricacies of contract, of inheritance, of marriage negotiations, are elevated into philosophical theatre: the comedy of estates and dowries becomes a comedy of existence itself. What is at stake is not merely who marries whom, but what it means to be an autonomous self in a society that is itself composed of transactions. In this sense, Congreve anticipates the great dialecticians of modernity, from Hegel’s master and slave to Kierkegaard’s knight of infinite resignation. For his characters, to speak is already to risk being undone, to seduce is already to become seduced.

The aesthetic daring of the play lies in its refusal to reconcile its cruelty with any sentimental balm. If later comedies, in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, would offer the comfort of domestic resolution, Congreve never permits the illusion that harmony is natural. His lovers may come to agreements, but these are agreements forged in combat, in concession, in ironic recognition of the other’s inviolable autonomy. The Restoration comedy’s libertine laughter had always been tinged with the bitterness of exposure, but Congreve transforms this into a vision of love as dialectical war. To love is to engage in perpetual negotiation, and the genius of his play is to show this not as degradation but as the very grandeur of being human.

One might situate Congreve, paradoxically, alongside the tragic canon. For though The Way of the World is a comedy, it possesses a tragic consciousness, an awareness of the limits of human freedom. Like Shakespeare’s Measure for Measure, it reveals the entanglement of desire, law, and morality, but unlike Shakespeare, Congreve refuses transcendence. There is no divine or cosmic judgment here; the only tribunal is that of wit itself, the judgment rendered by an audience of equals. In this sense, Congreve anticipates modernity far more than he resembles his Elizabethan ancestors. Where Hamlet delays, where Lear rages, Congreve’s lovers refine, negotiate, and endure. The grandeur of Shakespeare is cosmic; the grandeur of Congreve is social, but no less inexorable.

To understand Congreve’s originality, one must recall that comedy had long been regarded as a lesser form, subordinate to tragedy. Aristotle, in the Poetics, gave comedy scant attention, and the Western canon followed suit, treating comedy as diversion rather than destiny. Yet in Congreve we find comedy raised to the level of philosophy, no longer the mere exposure of folly but the revelation of the human condition in its most intricate social reality. He thus stands with Cervantes, with Molière, with Joyce, as one of those rare writers who make comedy the equal of tragedy in expressing the pathos of existence. The laughter of The Way of the World is not relief but recognition: we laugh because we see ourselves in the inexorable play of wit and desire, and because we know there is no escape from this play.

If Congreve seems, in our time, both remote and necessary, it is because he embodies the paradox of literature itself: the more his world recedes from ours, the more it illuminates what is eternal in us. The Restoration drawing room, with its codes of courtesy and cruelty, is long vanished, but the struggle of autonomy and intimacy, of contract and desire, remains. The play’s endurance lies in its ability to remind us that every age invents its own metaphors for power, and that language is always the battlefield where these metaphors become flesh. In Congreve, we encounter the purest distillation of this truth: that wit is not ornament but substance, not decoration but being itself.

The canon preserves Congreve not because he was fashionable, but because he achieved the unfashionable perfection of form. He wrote at the moment when his genre was about to vanish, and so he poured into it the intensity of finality. There is a sense in which The Way of the World is too perfect, too crystallized, to allow successors. After Congreve, the comedy of manners could only dilute itself into sentimentalism or parody. He exhausted the possibilities of his form, and thus ensured his place among those writers whose work is less a continuation than a culmination. To read him now is to feel the poignancy of this finality, and to recognize that literature, like love, is often at its most powerful when it knows it cannot continue.

In the grand architecture of Western literature, The Way of the World occupies the place of the consummate miniature: a chamber work of such polish that it stands beside the symphonies of Shakespeare and the novels of Cervantes without diminishment. Its aesthetic significance lies in its compression; its philosophical significance lies in its recognition that the social is never merely social, but the essence of human being. Congreve’s stylistic genius, his ability to fashion sentences that are at once crystalline and lethal, ensures that his work will endure, not merely as a document of its time but as a permanent contribution to our understanding of wit, love, and autonomy. To read him is to encounter not only the way of the world in 1700, but the way of the world in every age that knows the peril and glory of human desire.
